_note: US non-food products by PL category slug. Web research 2026-06-13 (Chewy, Petco, PetSmart, Amazon). verified:false until confirmed against real labels. Non-food pillars: A materials/build 35, B safety & evidence 25, C manufacturer credibility 25, D ergonomics & value 15. English content for /us/ pages.

przysmaki-i-gryzaki.name: Greenies Original Dental Treats
specs: wheat flour, wheat gluten, glycerin, gelatin, natural poultry flavor; soft/pliable
yes - ~47% less tartar, 40% less plaque in 28 days
przysmaki-i-gryzaki.verdict: The most vet-recommended dental treat in the US and one of the few carrying a VOHC seal, so its plaque and tartar benefit is clinically backed. The familiar trade-off applies: the wheat-gluten base isn't great nutrition, but as a soft, size-matched hygiene tool it earns its place.
cons: Calorie-dense - keep to ~10% of daily calories
Wheat-gluten base - not for grain-sensitive dogs
Give size-appropriate piece and supervise gulping

faq.q: Are Greenies safe?
faq.a: Modern Greenies are highly digestible and VOHC-accepted. The main cautions are giving the correct size for the dog, supervising so it isn't gulped, and counting the calories - not the old brittleness concerns of decades past.

faq.q: Do dental treats replace brushing?
faq.a: No. Daily brushing with enzymatic paste is the gold standard; a VOHC treat is a helpful add-on, not a substitute.

przysmaki-i-gryzaki.name: Virbac C.E.T. VeggieDent Fr3sh Chews
specs: corn starch, glycerin, soy protein isolate, rice flour, brewers yeast, pomegranate; Z-shape; no animal ingredients
yes - claims ~37% plaque, 70% tartar reduction; freshens breath
przysmaki-i-gryzaki.verdict: A vet-channel staple: VOHC-accepted, fully vegetarian, and with some of the strongest tartar-reduction claims in the category. The Z-shape encourages real chewing. It's pricier than mass-market chews and, like all of them, supplements rather than replaces brushing.
cons: Pricier than mass-market chews
Match size to dog to avoid swallowing whole
Calorie content - limit frequency

faq.q: Why is VeggieDent vegetarian?
faq.a: It uses plant proteins (soy, rice, corn) instead of meat, which suits dogs on novel-protein or elimination diets and owners avoiding animal ingredients - while still carrying the VOHC seal for dental efficacy.

faq.q: Is VeggieDent better than Greenies?
faq.a: Both are VOHC-accepted with similar starch-based bases. VeggieDent is vegetarian with higher headline tartar claims; Greenies is cheaper and more widely stocked. Either is a valid hygiene add-on to brushing.

przysmaki-i-gryzaki.name: Best Bully Sticks (6" Standard)
specs: thin and standard, 6" and bargain bags
yielding - far safer than antlers/bones for teeth
przysmaki-i-gryzaki.verdict: A single-ingredient chew that's genuinely digestible - unlike rawhide - and satisfying enough to keep a dog busy without the tooth-fracture risk of antlers or bones. Watch two things: the calories (it's pure beef) and the end nub, which is a choking hazard worth removing or holding.
pros: Yielding - safe for teeth vs antlers/bones
cons: Calorie-dense - cap at ~10% of daily calories
End nub is a choking hazard (remove last inches or use a holder)
Can have an odor; supervise

faq.q: Are bully sticks safer than rawhide?
faq.a: Generally yes - bully sticks are a single digestible muscle, whereas rawhide can swell and lodge in the gut. Both still need supervision and sizing to the dog.

faq.q: How do I stop my dog choking on the end?
faq.a: Take the stick away once it's down to a few inches, or use a bully-stick holder that grips the last piece so it can't be swallowed whole.

przysmaki-i-gryzaki.name: Purina DentaLife Daily Oral Care Chews
specs: rice, corn, wheat, glycerin, chicken; ridged, porous design
przysmaki-i-gryzaki.verdict: A VOHC-sealed, US-made dental chew whose porous, ridged texture is designed to reach more of the tooth surface. Affordable and clinically backed for tartar - with the same grain-based-nutrition caveat as every chew in this group. Treat it as hygiene, not food.
cons: Grain-based - not for grain-sensitive dogs
Counts toward daily calories
Supervise chewing

faq.q: DentaLife vs Greenies?
faq.a: Both are VOHC-accepted. DentaLife leans on a porous texture and a lower price; Greenies is softer and the most vet-recommended. The choice is largely preference - neither replaces brushing.

faq.q: Is a porous chew better for cleaning?
faq.a: The porous, ridged shape is intended to contact more tooth surface as the dog chews, which is part of why it earned VOHC acceptance. The benefit is real but modest - daily brushing still does more.

pielegnacja.name: Virbac Epi-Otic Advanced Ear Cleanser
specs: pH-neutral routine ear cleanser for dogs and cats
removes wax/debris; routine maintenance, not active infection
pielegnacja.verdict: A vet-trusted, pH-neutral cleanser whose anti-adhesive technology helps reduce recurrent ear infections - non-stinging and gentle for routine upkeep, and far cheaper at Chewy than at the clinic. The line is clear: this is maintenance, not medication. Active otitis needs a vet.
pros: Anti-adhesive tech helps reduce recurrent infections
Much cheaper online than at clinics
cons: A cleanser, not a medication - won't cure active otitis
Do not use on a perforated eardrum
Avoid over-cleaning healthy ears

faq.q: How often should I clean my dog's ears?
faq.a: Only when there's visible wax or debris - typically every few weeks depending on breed. Over-cleaning a healthy ear strips its protective barrier. Floppy, hairy-eared breeds need more frequent checks.

faq.q: How do I tell ear dirt from infection?
faq.a: Warning signs are strong odor, dark or pus-like discharge, redness, pain on touch, and head-shaking. Then a cleanser won't help - see a vet for targeted treatment.

pielegnacja.name: Earthbath Oatmeal & Aloe Dog Shampoo
specs: colloidal oatmeal (3%), aloe vera, coconut-derived cleansers, vitamins, allantoin
pH-balanced, soap-free, biodegradable, cruelty-free, made in USA
pielegnacja.verdict: A soothing, soap-free shampoo built around colloidal oatmeal and aloe - a sensible default for dry, itchy skin, correctly pH-balanced for dogs, biodegradable and US-made. The fragrance-free version suits reactive dogs. It cleans and soothes; it doesn't treat infections or parasites.
cons: Not medicated - won't treat infections or fleas
Avoid eyes
Some find the scent faint

faq.q: Is oatmeal shampoo good for an itchy dog?
faq.a: Colloidal oatmeal soothes and moisturizes mild dry, itchy skin and is a good everyday choice. But persistent itching, scaling or odor points to an underlying issue (allergy, infection, parasites) that needs a vet, not a cosmetic wash.

faq.q: Can I use human shampoo instead?
faq.a: No - dog skin is more neutral (around pH 7) than ours, and human shampoos disrupt that barrier. Use a dog-formulated, pH-balanced product.

pielegnacja.name: Hertzko Self-Cleaning Slicker Brush
specs: fine bent-wire bristles, one-button retractable cleaning, anti-slip handle
tangles and loose undercoat on short and long coats
pielegnacja.verdict: A best-selling, inexpensive slicker whose one-button bristle retraction makes cleanup effortless. It handles tangles and loose undercoat across coat types. As with any slicker, the caution is pressure - fine wires can scratch or cause 'slicker burn' if you bear down.
pros: One-button bristle retraction for easy cleanup
Works on tangles and loose undercoat
cons: Fine wires can scratch or cause 'slicker burn' if pressed too hard
Not ideal for heavy matting (de-mat first)
Check bristle tips for sharpness over time

faq.q: How do I use a slicker brush safely?
faq.a: Light, short strokes, letting the pins do the work - never press hard. For mats, hold the skin taut and tease them out gradually rather than dragging through.

faq.q: Is a slicker enough for my dog's coat?
faq.a: For most short-to-medium coats and surface tangles, yes. Heavy double coats also need an undercoat rake or deshedding tool to reach the dense underlayer.

pielegnacja.name: FURminator Undercoat deShedding Tool
specs: stainless-steel undercoat deShedding edge with FUREjector button
reduces loose shedding hair up to ~90% on double-coated breeds
NOT for single-coated breeds (Poodle, Maltese, Yorkie, Bichon, many Doodles)
pielegnacja.verdict: Highly effective - but only on the right coat. On double-coated breeds (Husky, Lab, Golden, Corgi) it cuts loose shedding by up to 90%. On single-coated dogs it does harm, removing and damaging the coat. The score reflects that real risk of misuse.
pros: Cuts loose shedding up to ~90% on double coats
cons: NOT for single-coated breeds - removes/damages the coat
Over-use thins coat and irritates skin
Don't use on matted or wet fur

faq.q: Can I use a FURminator on a Poodle or Doodle?
faq.a: No. Poodles, Doodles, Maltese and similar are single-coated (no undercoat) - the FURminator cuts and damages their coat. Use a slicker brush and comb instead.

faq.q: Which dogs is it for?
faq.a: Heavy-shedding double-coated breeds: Husky, Malamute, German Shepherd, Labrador, Golden, Corgi. Use once or twice a week on dry, brushed-out fur with light pressure.

akcesoria.name: Ruffwear Front Range Harness
specs: 300-denier ripstop shell, closed-cell foam padding, knit-mesh lining, reflective trim, ID pocket
front (anti-pull) and back clips; four points of adjustment
akcesoria.verdict: The everyday benchmark: a comfortable, well-padded harness with both a front anti-pull clip and a back clip, four adjustment points and reflective trim. Premium-priced and worth fitting carefully - for determined escape artists a 3-point/anti-escape design is safer.
pros: Well-padded and comfortable for daily wear
cons: Premium price
Needs correct sizing to avoid chafing or escape
Not a dedicated anti-escape design

faq.q: Front clip or back clip?
faq.a: Use the front clip for training and pullers - it steers the dog's chest toward you, reducing pulling. Use the back clip for relaxed walks and for dogs that already walk on a loose leash.

faq.q: How should it fit?
faq.a: Snug but not tight - about two fingers under each strap, the chest panel clear of the armpits, and no rotation when the dog moves. Measure girth behind the front legs before buying.

akcesoria.name: PetSafe Easy Walk No-Pull Harness
specs: front-clip no-pull training harness with martingale chest loop
nylon webbing, front D-ring, quick-snap shoulder and belly buckles; sits across chest, not throat
management aid for pullers; not a substitute for training
akcesoria.verdict: An affordable, widely available no-pull tool: the front clip and chest loop steer a puller's body sideways, reducing pulling without choking. It works as a management aid, but the girth-only strap can chafe and slightly restricts shoulder reach, so it isn't for running or sport - and it doesn't replace loose-leash training.
pros: Front clip + chest strap reduce pulling without choking
cons: Can loosen, slip or rotate if not snug
Girth-only strap may chafe armpits
Restricts shoulder reach - not for running/sport
A management aid, not a training substitute

faq.q: Does a no-pull harness stop pulling for good?
faq.a: It manages pulling in the moment by redirecting the dog, but it doesn't teach loose-leash walking on its own. Pair it with reward-based training so the dog learns the behavior, not just wears the gear.

faq.q: Easy Walk or a Y-harness?
faq.a: For active anti-pull control the Easy Walk's front clip helps; for everyday comfort and free shoulder movement a padded Y-harness (e.g. Ruffwear Front Range, which also has a front clip) is gentler on the gait.

akcesoria.name: Mendota Pet Rope Snap Leash
specs: solid-braid UV-coated rope, oil-tanned leather splices, heavy brass snap; waterproof, washable
3/8" (≤50 lb), 1/2" (>50 lb); 4 ft, 6 ft, 15 ft training
akcesoria.verdict: A British-style braided rope lead, US-made and built to last: weatherproof, colorfast, machine-washable, with a comfortable grip and quality brass hardware. The one caution is friction - bare-handed, a hard pull can let the rope burn through your grip, and there's no padded handle.
cons: Rope can cause friction burn on a hard pull
No padded handle
Pricier than basic nylon leads

faq.q: What leash should I avoid for everyday walks?
faq.a: Retractable leads - they teach pulling (the dog only moves with the cord under tension), give poor close control near roads, and the thin cord can burn or cut. A fixed-length rope or padded lead is safer.

faq.q: Which rope diameter do I need?
faq.a: Use 3/8" for dogs up to about 50 lb and 1/2" for larger or stronger dogs; pick 4–6 ft for walks and a 15 ft version for recall and long-line training.

zabawki.name: KONG Classic / KONG Extreme
specs: natural rubber; Classic (red) for moderate chewers, Extreme (black) for power chewers
hollow, fillable and freezable; erratic bounce
zabawki.verdict: The category benchmark and the safe-material standard. Stuffed and frozen it's the best boredom-buster in the business, and the black Extreme covers strong chewers. Size it correctly - too small is a choking risk - and discard once chewed through; even Extreme isn't truly indestructible.
pros: Excellent enrichment when stuffed and frozen
Natural rubber - Extreme version for power chewers
cons: Wrong size is a choking/swallowing risk
Even Extreme isn't fully indestructible - discard when worn
Clean stuffing residue to avoid mold

faq.q: Classic (red) or Extreme (black)?
faq.a: Red Classic suits most dogs; black Extreme is the tougher rubber for dogs that destroy the Classic. Puppies have their own softer version. All are safe, springy rubber that won't fracture teeth like antlers.

faq.q: What size KONG do I need?
faq.a: Size up - it must be too big to fit whole in the mouth. For large dogs choose XL/XXL even if a Large looks adequate.

zabawki.name: West Paw Zogoflex Hurley / Tux
specs: Zogoflex - FDA-compliant, non-toxic, latex/BPA-free; buoyant, dishwasher-safe, recyclable
zabawki.verdict: Tough, floatable and non-toxic, with a guarantee against chewer damage - the Hurley for fetch and water, the stuffable Tux for enrichment. Not indestructible (replace when punctured) and not a hard gnaw bone, but among the safest tough toys you can buy.
pros: Tough and buoyant - fetch and water play
cons: Not indestructible - replace when punctured
Pick the right size for chewer strength
Pliable plastic, not a hard long-term gnaw bone

faq.q: How does the guarantee work?
faq.a: West Paw will replace a Zogoflex toy a dog destroys - a strong signal of confidence in the material. In practice it's tough enough that few qualify, but the cover is there.

faq.q: Zogoflex or KONG for a heavy chewer?
faq.a: Both are top tier. KONG Extreme is firmer and cheaper; Zogoflex is more pliable, dishwasher-safe, floats, and carries a damage guarantee. For dogs that crack teeth on hard objects, the more pliable option is safer.

zabawki.name: Chuckit! Ultra Ball
specs: thick natural high-bounce rubber - won't abrade enamel like tennis-ball felt; buoyant
Small (2") to XXL; Medium 2.5" (20–60 lb), Large 3" (60–100 lb)
zabawki.verdict: A durable, high-bounce fetch ball that floats and pairs with the Chuckit launcher for long, hands-free throws. Crucially, its smooth rubber doesn't sand down enamel the way tennis-ball felt does. Size it up - a too-small ball is a serious airway hazard - and treat it as a fetch toy, not a chew.
pros: Durable rubber, high bounce for fetch
Smooth surface - safe for enamel (unlike tennis felt)
cons: Too-small ball is a serious choking hazard - size up for big dogs
Not a chew toy - strong chewers can tear chunks
Supervise play

faq.q: Why are tennis balls bad for dogs' teeth?
faq.a: Tennis-ball felt is abrasive - designed for hard courts - and picks up grit, so chewing and carrying it sands down enamel, which doesn't grow back. The Ultra Ball's smooth rubber avoids this.

faq.q: What size ball does my dog need?
faq.a: Bigger than the mouth can engulf. For Labs and Shepherds use at least Large (3"); a ball that can lodge in the throat is a leading fetch-related emergency.

zabawki.name: Outward Hound - Nina Ottosson Puzzle
specs: durable, dishwasher-safe composite; sliding/flipping compartments - not a chew
zabawki.verdict: Strong mental enrichment with clear difficulty tiers so the dog can progress, while slowing fast eaters and easing boredom. Easy to clean and food-safe - just remember it's a supervised activity, not a chew: determined dogs can break off and swallow plastic pieces.
pros: Strong mental enrichment; slows fast eaters
Clear difficulty levels to progress the dog
cons: Not a chew - dogs may break and swallow plastic
Some solve easy levels fast (rotate/level up)
Needs supervision and resetting

faq.q: Which level should I start at?
faq.a: Level 1 (e.g. the Brick) for first-timers. Build confidence with easy wins, then move up only once the dog solves the current puzzle quickly and reliably.

faq.q: Can I leave my dog alone with a puzzle?
faq.a: No - supervise. Determined dogs can chew off and swallow the sliding pieces, which risks an intestinal blockage. Put it away after the session.
